questions.prompt: In which sentence can the relative pronoun be correctly left out?

options.text: The man who called you is waiting outside.
The bus which goes to the centre is late.
The film that we watched was boring.
The woman who lives upstairs is a nurse.

questions.explanation: You can drop the pronoun only when it is the object: in (c), "we" follows, so that is the object — The film we watched. In (a), (b) and (d) the pronoun is the subject (a verb follows: called, goes, lives), so it must stay.

questions.prompt: Combine into one sentence: "I'll never forget the day. We won the championship that day."

options.text: I'll never forget the day which we won the championship.
I'll never forget the day when we won the championship.
I'll never forget the day who we won the championship.
I'll never forget the day whose we won the championship.

questions.explanation: For a time, use when (= on which): the day when we won. Bare "which" can't carry the meaning "on that day" here (a), "who" is for people (c), and "whose" shows possession and needs a noun after it (d).

questions.prompt: Complete: "I apologised to the customer ___ order had been lost."

options.text: whose
who's
who
which

questions.explanation: whose shows possession (their order): the customer whose order had been lost. The common trap is "who's", which means "who is" (b); "who" needs a verb after it (c); and "which" is for things, not a person (d).

options.text: The parcel that arrived this morning is for you.
The girl whose phone rang left the room.
The diner where we met has closed down.
The engineer designed the bridge is from Sfax.

questions.explanation: In (d) the subject pronoun is missing: it should be the engineer who designed the bridge. When the pronoun is the subject (a verb follows: designed) you cannot drop it. (a), (b) and (c) are all correct.

questions.prompt: Complete: "The scientist ___ discovered the cure works in a lab ___ is funded by the state."

options.text: which … who
who … which
whose … where
where … that

questions.explanation: The scientist is a person (who discovered), and the lab is a thing that is the subject of "is funded" (which). So: who … which. The other pairs mismatch the pronoun to the noun — a person is never "which" and a lab is never "who".

questions.prompt: Read: "My uncle, a man that everyone trusts, runs a shop where you can buy anything you need." Which sentence is true?

options.text: Everyone trusts my uncle.
Nobody trusts my uncle.
The shop sells only food.
My uncle works in a bank.

questions.explanation: "a man that everyone trusts" means everyone trusts him, so (a) is true. (b) reverses it; the clause "where you can buy anything" shows the shop is not food-only (c); and it is a shop, not a bank (d).
